title: Why Is My Monstera Turning Yellow and Black?
excerpt: Discover the most common reasons your Monstera leaves are turning yellow and black, and learn exactly how to fix each problem to restore your plant to health.
content:
Why Is My Monstera Turning Yellow and Black?

Few things are more alarming than walking over to your beloved Monstera and noticing its gorgeous leaves going yellow, black, or a troubling combination of both. Before you panic, take a breath — most of the time, these symptoms are completely fixable once you know what's causing them.

In this guide, we'll walk you through every likely culprit behind a yellowing and blackening Monstera, plus the exact steps you need to take to bring it back to its lush, thriving best.

Understanding the Difference: Yellow vs. Black Discoloration

The color and pattern of discoloration on your Monstera leaves are actually incredibly useful clues. Yellow leaves typically signal issues with water, light, or nutrients, while black spots or edges usually point to more serious problems like root rot, bacterial infection, or cold damage. When you see both together, it often means a problem has been developing for a while.

Pay attention to where the discoloration appears — is it spreading from the edges inward? Starting as spots in the center? Affecting only older lower leaves? Each pattern tells a different story.

The Most Common Causes of Yellow and Black Monstera Leaves

1. Overwatering and Root Rot

This is the number one reason Monstera leaves turn yellow and then black. When your plant sits in soggy soil for too long, the roots suffocate and begin to rot. A rotting root system can't deliver water or nutrients to the leaves, causing them to yellow first and then develop black, mushy patches.

How to fix it:

  • Remove the plant from its pot and inspect the roots. Healthy roots are firm and white or tan; rotten roots are brown, black, and mushy.
  • Trim away all rotten roots with clean, sterilized scissors.
  • Repot into fresh, well-draining soil and a pot with pots with drainage holes.
  • Allow the top 2 inches of soil to dry out completely before watering again.

If you suspect overwatering is an ongoing issue, our guide on how often to water a Monstera can help you dial in the perfect watering schedule.

2. Underwatering

On the flip side, a thirsty Monstera will also show yellow leaves — though in this case, the leaves tend to look dry, crispy, and curled rather than soft and mushy. Black tips can develop as the plant's tissue dries out and dies.

How to fix it:

  • Push your finger 2 inches into the soil. If it's bone dry, it's time to water thoroughly.
  • Water slowly and deeply until it drains freely from the bottom of the pot.
  • Establish a consistent watering routine — most Monsteras need watering every 1–2 weeks depending on light and season.

3. Bacterial Leaf Spot

If your Monstera has dark, water-soaked spots with yellow halos around them, bacterial leaf spot is likely the cause. This infection spreads quickly in humid conditions and thrives when water sits on the leaves.

How to fix it:

  • Remove and dispose of all affected leaves immediately — do not compost them.
  • Avoid misting your Monstera directly, and ensure good air circulation around the plant.
  • Apply a copper-based fungicide or bactericide as directed on the label.
  • Isolate the plant from your other houseplants until the issue resolves.

4. Fungal Infections

Fungal issues like root rot fungus or leaf blight can cause a combination of yellow and black discoloration. Fungal problems are most common in poorly ventilated spaces with high humidity and inconsistent watering.

How to fix it:

  • Improve air circulation by moving the plant to a less crowded spot.
  • Reduce humidity if it's consistently above 70% in your space.
  • Treat with a diluted neem oil solution or a commercial fungicide.

For more on protecting your plants from common infections, check out our article on common Monstera diseases and how to treat them.

5. Cold Damage and Temperature Stress

Monsteras are tropical plants that hate the cold. Exposure to temperatures below 50°F (10°C), cold drafts from windows, or air conditioning vents can cause black patches to appear rapidly, often starting at the leaf edges and tips.

How to fix it:

  • Move your Monstera away from cold windows, exterior doors, and air conditioning units.
  • Keep your plant in temperatures between 65–85°F (18–29°C) for optimal health.
  • Remove blackened leaves as they won't recover, but the rest of the plant will bounce back once conditions improve.

6. Nutrient Deficiencies

A lack of key nutrients — particularly nitrogen, magnesium, or iron — can cause widespread yellowing across the leaves. Over time, affected areas may darken and die off if the deficiency isn't addressed.

How to fix it:

  • Feed your Monstera with a balanced, water-soluble balanced liquid fertilizer (such as a 20-20-20 formula) once a month during spring and summer.
  • Avoid fertilizing in fall and winter when the plant's growth slows.
  • If you suspect a specific deficiency, a soil test can help pinpoint the problem.

7. Pests

Sap-sucking pests like spider mites, scale insects, and thrips can cause yellow stippling and spotting on Monstera leaves. As damage accumulates, affected areas can turn brown or black and die off.

How to fix it:

  • Inspect the undersides of leaves closely for tiny bugs, webbing, or sticky residue.
  • Wipe leaves down with a damp cloth and treat with insecticidal soap or neem oil.
  • Repeat treatment every 5–7 days for at least three weeks to break the pest life cycle.

Want to learn more about identifying and eliminating common invaders? Visit our guide on identifying Monstera pests.

How to Prevent Yellow and Black Leaves in the Future

Once you've addressed the immediate issue, here's how to keep your Monstera looking its best long-term:

  1. Water correctly: Always check soil moisture before watering rather than following a rigid schedule.
  2. Choose the right pot: Always use a pot with drainage holes to prevent waterlogging.
  3. Provide bright, indirect light: A few feet back from a south or east-facing window is ideal.
  4. Feed regularly in the growing season: Monthly fertilizing from spring through summer keeps nutrients topped up.
  5. Inspect leaves regularly: Catching pest or disease problems early makes them far easier to treat.

Should You Remove Yellow and Black Leaves?

Yes — in most cases, you should remove leaves that are more than 50% damaged. These leaves won't recover, and removing them helps redirect the plant's energy toward healthy new growth. Always use clean, sharp scissors or pruning shears, and wipe the blades with rubbing alcohol before and after cutting to prevent spreading any potential infection.

Final Thoughts

A Monstera with yellow and black leaves is sending you a clear message that something in its environment needs to change. The good news is that with a little detective work and prompt action, most Monsteras recover beautifully. Work through the causes above systematically, make the necessary adjustments, and give your plant a little time — it will reward you with the lush, tropical growth you fell in love with.

For more Monstera care tips, explore our full Monstera care guide on LeafLogic.
